coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of fluorescence sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer on amine-modified carbon quantum dots for fast and highly sensitive and selective detection of tannic acid in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Sistani, Shabnam
Shekarchizadeh, Hajar
coredata.dc:description: Developing a rapid and accurate method for tannic acid (TA) detection and measurement is necessary due to its extensive applications in the food industry. In this work, a fluorescence sensor with a low limit of detection was synthesized for TA for the first time. First, amine-modified carbon quantum dots (a-CQDs) with high-quantum yield were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. A layer of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was then placed on a-CQDs by the surface printing method to increase the sensor selectivity. The mechanism of TA detection by the prepared a-CQDs/MIPs was quenching the fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs in the presence of TA due to the transfer of electrons from the TA to the a-CQDs. The linear range of the sensor response was at the TA concentration of 1–200 nmol L−1 and its detection limit was 0.6 nmol L−1 under optimal conditions. Finally, the sensor was used to measure TA in grape juice, green tea, and black tea samples using the recovery method. Recovery values between 97.4 and 103.6% and RSDs less than 3.8% indicated the high potential of the prepared sensor for TA analysis in complex food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Hydrothermal
Fluorescence sensor
Surface printing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) Research Council for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A): TEM image of a-CQDs, (B): particle size distribution diagram of a-CQDs, (C): XRD pattern of a-CQDs, (D): FTIR spectrum of a-CQDs, (E): UV–Vis absorption spectrum of a-CQDs, and (F): Fluorescence emission spectra of a-CQDs.
FESEM images of (A): a-CQDs/MIPs, (B): a-CQDs/NIPs, and (C): FTIR spectra of a-CQDs/MIPs (a), and a-CQDs/NIPs (b).
(A): UV–Vis absorption spectra of extracted mixture after each wash, and (B): fluorescence spectra of a-CQDs/MIPs after each wash.
(A): UV–Vis absorption spectra of TA and a-CQDs/MIPs, (B): UV–Vis absorption spectra of TA and fluorescence spectra of a-CQDs/MIPs.

(A): Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of sensor (in the presence of 40 nmol L−1 TA), (B): Effect of a-CQDs/MIPs amount on fluorescence intensity of sensor (in the presence of 40 nmol L−1 TA at pH = 7), (C): Effect of incubation time on fluorescence intensity of sensor (500 mg L−1 of a-CQDs/MIPs in the presence of 40 nmol L−1 TA at pH = 7).
Capital letters show significant differences between sensor responses in each plot (p < 0.05).
Fluorescence emission spectra of a-CQDs/MIPs (A), and a-CQDs/NIPs (B) in the presence of different concentrations of TA.
Calibration curve of TA concentration versus the fluorescence intensities of a-CQDs/MIPs (A), and a-CQDs/NIPs (B).
(A): Fluorescence emission stability of a-CQDs/MIPs, (B): Fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs in the presence of 40 nmol L−1 TA and 200 nmol L−1 of different interfering species.
A-G show significant differences between a-CQDs/MIPs responses (p < 0.05) and Aˈ-Jˈ show significant differences between a-CQDs/NIPs responses (p < 0.05).
Recovery test in grape juice, black tea, and green tea.
Linear range and detection limit of this method with several reported methods of TA measurement.

row.entry: Grape juice, Black tea, Green tea

head.ce:title: Fabrication of fluorescence sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer on amine-modified carbon quantum dots for fast and highly sensitive and selective detection of tannic acid in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, College of Agriculture, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, College of Agriculture, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a rapid and accurate method for tannic acid (TA) detection and measurement is necessary due to its extensive applications in the food industry. In this work, a fluorescence sensor with a low limit of detection was synthesized for TA for the first time. First, amine-modified carbon quantum dots (a-CQDs) with high-quantum yield were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. A layer of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was then placed on a-CQDs by the surface printing method to increase the sensor selectivity. The mechanism of TA detection by the prepared a-CQDs/MIPs was quenching the fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs in the presence of TA due to the transfer of electrons from the TA to the a-CQDs. The linear range of the sensor response was at the TA concentration of 1–200 nmol L−1 and its detection limit was 0.6 nmol L−1 under optimal conditions. Finally, the sensor was used to measure TA in grape juice, green tea, and black tea samples using the recovery method. Recovery values between 97.4 and 103.6% and RSDs less than 3.8% indicated the high potential of the prepared sensor for TA analysis in complex food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Tannic acid (TA) is a water-soluble phenolic compound found in many plant origin foods, such as cereals, legumes, fruits, wine, and tea [1]. TA has unique properties such as anti-cancer, anti-mutation, and antioxidant activity [2]. Its applications in the food industry including stabilizing agent in beer, the clarifying agent in beverages, flavoring agent, and increasing food shelf life. Its safe dose as an additive is 10–400 μg.

TA is considered as a criterion for evaluating food quality, also, to be effective on taste [3–6]. On the other hand, continuous or large amounts of TA consumption reduce the absorption of iron and other minerals and reduce the reaction of digestive enzymes [7]. The positive or negative effect of TA on human health depends on the dosage. Therefore, it is necessary to obtain an accurate and rapid measurement method to detect and determine the amount of TA in food.

So far, various methods have been used to detect TA including chemoluminescence [8,9], electrochemistry [10], photoelectrochemistry [11], spectrophotometry [12], resonance Rayleigh scattering [13], differential pulse voltammetry [6] and fluorescence spectroscopy [14,15]. Fluorescence spectroscopy is more attractive among these methods due to its higher sensitivity, simplicity, and cheapness.

Organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, which are mainly used for fluorescence analysis, have disadvantages that limit their widespread use. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new generation of carbon nanomaterials that have received great attention due to their properties such as easy synthesis, excellent solubility, low toxicity, ease of operation, and unique luminescence. Unlike organic dyes, CQDs have high optical stability. They also have the advantage of low toxicity and good biocompatibility over other quantum dots [16,17]. Among the various methods of synthesizing CQDs, the hydrothermal method is one of the most popular synthesis methods due to its simplicity of synthesis method, uniform particle size, environmentally friendly, and high quantum yield [18]. CQDs mainly have electron-extracting groups such as carboxylic acid and epoxy groups, which reduce electron density and produce relatively low quantum yield CQDs. While using CQDs in detection, a high quantum yield is required [19]. One of the most common methods of increasing quantum yield is a surface chemical modification with amine groups. In this method, the epoxy groups and carboxylic acid, which are on the CQDs surface and cause the non-radiative combination of the electron-hole pairs, are replaced with the CONHR and CNHR groups. New groups stabilize the emission energy trap on the surface and increase quantum yield [20].

Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) technology is one way to improve the selectivity of CQDs. In other words, a combination of high sensitivity detection using fluorescent CQDs and high selectivity detection using MIP leads to the new sensors with high sensitivity and accuracy [21]. MIP is a synthetic polymer whose operating mechanism is according to the lock and key theory [22]. MIP is synthesized through copolymerization of the functional monomer and cross-linker in the presence of the template molecule. The template molecule is separated after polymerization and a polymer containing a cavity is obtained, which is complementary to the template molecule in terms of shape, size, and functional groups [23]. MIP advantages include high selectivity, easy synthesis, high strength, and low cost. MIP, despite its proper detection function, lacks an output signal during analysis and detection. Therefore, it needs to be used with other confirmation methods such as fluorescent materials [24]. The surface printing method among the MIP synthesis methods has been used in recent years due to its easy preparation, high selectivity, suitable adsorption capacity, easy bonding, and separation of the template. Surface imprinting is considered one of the solid phase printing methods during which a layer of MIP is formed around the solid mediums such as nanoparticles. The small size of the CQDs and their large surface-to-volume ratio improve the bonding capacity and accelerate the bonding and separation of the template molecules [25].

This work aims to design a new fluorescent sensor to detect TA. As mentioned, several methods have been reported to measure TA, but this work is the first method to detect TA using a combination of MIP technology and CQDs. For this purpose, amine-modified carbon quantum dots (a-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment. Then, a layer of MIP was applied to a-CQDs by surface printing. The characteristics of a-CQDs and nanocomposites in terms of morphology, optical properties, functional groups as well as selectivity of sensor in the presence of interfering factors are investigated. Finally, the performance of the synthesized sensor is investigated in real samples.

Grapes, green tea, and black tea were purchased from the local market of Isfahan. Citric acid, cyclohexane, Triton X-100, ammonium hydroxide, acetone, ethanol, tannic acid, acetonitrile, quinine, glucose, sucrose, tartaric acid, gallic acid, glycine, silver nitrate (AgNO3), calcium chloride (CaCl2), iron chloride (FeCl3), potassium chloride (KCl), and sodium ascorbate were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Polyethylenimine (PEI), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysylane (APTES) were purchased from Sigma (London, UK). Acetic acid (0.04 mol L−1), boric acid (0.04 mol L−1), and phosphoric acid (0.04 mol L−1) solutions were used to prepare the Britton Robinson buffer and the pH was adjusted by sodium hydroxide solution (0.2 mol L−1). Double distilled water was used for all tests and preparation of solutions.

Hydrothermal treatment was used to synthesize a-CQDs in which citric acid was used as a carbon source, and branched PEI was used to modify CQDs with amine groups. 0.945 g PEI and 0.1 g citric acid were weighed and added to 30 ml distilled water and stirred for 1 h to complete dissolution. The solution was autoclaved at 180 °C for 20 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, a clear yellow solution containing a-CQDs was obtained, which was kept in a dark glass container at 4 °C until use [26].

The absorption spectra were recorded using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Hach Lange, USA). 50 μl of a-CQDs in 5 ml distilled water was used. The absorption spectrum of the sample was recorded between 200 and 600 nm. A spectroﬂuorometer (Shimadzu RF–5000, Japan) was used to study the emission spectrum and excitation-dependent behavior. For this purpose, a suspension of 10 μl a-CQDs in 5 ml of distilled water was used. The fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded in the excitation wavelength range of 310–430 nm.

TEM image was obtained to study the morphology of a-CQDs (Philips CM120, Netherlands). 10 μl diluted solution of a-CQDs was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid and vacuum-dried. The sample was placed between the object's lens poles using a retaining rod, and images were taken with specific magnifications. A particle size distribution diagram was drawn using Image J analysis software.

An X-ray diffractometer (Asenware AW-XDM300, China) was used to investigate the crystalline structure of the synthesized a-CQDs. The a-CQDs suspension was placed on the device housing. Then, the reflected beam was measured by radiation from a copper anticathode with CuKα = 1.54178 Å wavelength, step time of 1s, step size of 0.05, a voltage of 40 kV, and current of 30 mA in the range of 2θ = 5–60°. XRD analysis was performed using Xpert Highscore Plus software.

FTIR spectra were recorded using (Brucker Tensor 27, Germany) to confirm the synthesis of a-CQDs and study their properties. A tablet containing KBr and sample (1:10) was made and placed inside the device. FTIR analysis was performed in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 at a scan speed of 2 mm/s.

Zeta potential analysis was performed to determine the electric charge of CQDs using a zeta sizer (Horiba, Japan). The a-CQDs suspension was sonicated for 10 min. Then, the measurement was performed at 25 °C.

Quinine sulfate was selected as the reference fluorophore to measure quantum yield. Quinine sulfate diluted in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution has a quantum yield of 0.54. The absorbance of samples was recorded at a wavelength of 350 nm. Quantum yield was calculated using the following equation:
Eq.1
∅ X
=
∅
S T
(
I X
I
S T
)
(
A
S T
A X
)
(
ŋ X
2
ŋ
S T
2
)
where X index refers to the a-CQDs and the ST index represents the standard reference material. Ø, I, A, and ŋ represent quantum yield, the integrated fluorescence emission intensity of the sample, adsorption, and solvent refractive index, respectively [27].

The reverse microemulsion method was used to synthesize nanocomposite (a-CQDs/MIPs). Initially, 7.5 ml cyclohexane was added to 1.8 ml Triton X-100 and stirred at room temperature for 15 min. Then 100 μl ammonium hydroxide, 400 μl a-CQDs, and 50 μl TEOS were added to the reaction vessel and stirred for 2 h. Next, 5 mg TA and 20 μl APTES were added to the solution and stirred at room temperature for 12 h. Then, 20 ml acetone was used to break the microemulsion. The reaction solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The precipitate was washed with a mixture solvent of ethanol and acetonitrile (8:2 v/v) until no TA in the wash solvent could be detected by UV–Vis spectroscopy. Finally, the synthesized nanocomposite was dried at ambient temperature. The non-imprinted polymer (NIP) on the surface of a-CQDs (a-CQDs/NIPs) was synthesized similarly to the a-CQDs-MIPs synthesis steps, except that TA was not added [28].

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Philips XI30, Netherlands) was used to study a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposites morphology. FTIR spectrum was also recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 to confirm the MIP synthesis.

The parameters affecting sensor responses (pH, amount of a-CQDs/MIPs, and incubation time) were optimized to determine the best conditions for sensor response to the analyte. The univariate method was used to optimize different variables [29].

Briton Robinson buffer in the pH range of 4–11 was used to optimize the pH. A solution containing 500 mg L−1 a-CQDs/MIPs with different pH was prepared in the presence of 40 nmol L−1 TA, and the sensor response was recorded after 120 s. The response of various amounts of nanocomposite (200–600 mg L−1) to 40 nmol L−1 TA at pH = 7 after 120s was recorded to evaluate the effect of a-CQDs/MIPs. A solution containing 500 mg L−1 a-CQDs/MIPs was prepared in the presence of 40 nmol L−1 TA at pH = 7 to evaluate the effect of time. The sensor response was then recorded at a time of 0–240 s.

1.5 mg a-CQDs/MIPs was added to 3 ml of Britton Robinson buffer (pH = 7) and sonicated for 10 min. The fluorescence intensity of this solution was measured in the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and the emission wavelength of 440 nm in the absence of TA (F0). Then different volumes of TA standard solutions were added to the cell and their fluorescence intensities were recorded (F). Finally, F0-F values were used to evaluate the response of the sensor at different concentrations of TA. All the above steps were performed for a-CQDs/NIPs.

The detection limit is the lowest concentration of analyte that the sensor can detect and reflects the accuracy of the synthesized sensor. The following equation was used to calculate the detection limit, in which Sb represents the blank standard deviation signal (n = 6), and m is the slope of the calibration curve [30].
Eq.2
L O D = 3 S b
/ m

The selectivity of the MIP was determined by the imprinting factor (IF). The IF is a parameter to describe the MIP ability to identify a template. Detection of template molecules by MIP depends on the imprinted cavities, which are similar in shape, size, and chemical functional groups to the template molecule [31]. After calculating K for both nanocomposites; the imprinting factor was calculated using the following equation:
Eq.3
I F =
K
M I P s
K
N I P s

Reproducibility of the sensor was determined by measuring the response of three different a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposites to TA concentration of 40 nmol L−1, and then relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. In addition, the sensor response was measured for two concentrations of TA (n = 3) by the same a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite, and RSD was calculated. The stability of the sensor was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity changes of the a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite over time. The a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite response was measured every 5 min for 1 h. Then the RSD was calculated.

The grapes were washed and homogenized with a mixer. 10 ml of grape extract was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was used without any pretreatment. 2 g green tea and black tea were weighed, and each was soaked separately in 200 ml boiling water for 10 min and cooled to ambient temperature. The filter paper was used to filter the tea extracts. Each sample was diluted (10-fold) with buffer solution (pH = 7). A standard increase (recovery) method was used to detect TA in the samples. 300 μl of samples with specified concentrations of TA was added to 3 ml suspension containing 1.5 mg a-CQDs/MIPs with pH = 7. Finally, TA was measured using fluorescence measurements.

All experiments were done with three replicates. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by the least significant difference (LSD) test at p < 0.05 was done on the experimental data using SAS 9.4 software.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to study the morphology and size of synthesized a-CQDs. Fig. 1 A and B shows TEM image and particle size distribution diagram of a-CQDs, respectively. According to Fig. 1, the nanoparticles are quasi-spherical, and their average size is 4.9 ± 1.2 nm. Regarding the quasi-spherical shape and the size below 10 nm, which are the prominent features of CQDs, the obtained image confirms the synthesis of CQDs [32].

The XRD pattern of a-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1C. A wide peak at 2Ɵ = 28° and a weak peak at 2Ɵ = 41° are related to the crystal plates (002) and (100) in the carbon phase, respectively. According to the results, it can be stated that the a-CQDs are amorphous particles as a result of irregularity of carbon atoms and also the very close and compact presence of carbon atoms in the a-CQDs [33].

FTIR analysis was performed to determine the functional groups of a-CQDs. The FTIR spectrum of a-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1D. According to the figure, the wide absorption band at 3330 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration -OH/-NH. The absorption bands at 2922 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 identify the stretching vibrations of C-H and C=O, respectively. The broadband at the center of the wavenumber of 3330 cm−1 and its spread over a wide wavenumber range indicates the presence of large amounts of amine groups at the surface of a-CQDs [34].

The amount of zeta potential of a-CQDs was −1.5 ± 0.5. The negative charge indicates the presence of carboxyl functional groups on the surface of a-CQDs. However, the low negative charge is related to the presence of amine groups on the surface [35].

Fig. 1E shows the absorption spectra of a-CQDs. There are two peaks at 240 and 360 nm. The first peak is related to the transfer of π-π∗ from the aromatic ring, and the second peak is related to the transfer of n-π∗ from the carbonyl groups [26].

The fluorescence emission spectra of a-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 430 nm are shown in Fig. 1F. The fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs changes by the excitation wavelength changes. Excitation dependent behavior is a prominent feature of CQDs and confirms its synthesis [32]. Since the maximum emission wavelength was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, this wavelength was considered the optimal wavelength for all experiments.

The quantum yield of a-CQDs was calculated to be 24%. Synthesis of a-CQDs by the hydrothermal method was performed in a one-step reaction. PEI is a functional polycationic and water-soluble polymer with the first, second, and third types of amine groups. Quantum yield was significantly increased and caused a higher sensitive sensor because of the presence of amine groups on the surface of a-CQDs. The presence of amine groups stabilizes emissive traps on the surface of carbon quantum dots. PEI also acts as a protective layer and provides higher stability of a-CQDs against light and metals. Other advantages of surface modification with PEI include increased solubility of a-CQDs in aqueous media [36]. Although the mechanism of the a-CQDs synthesis is not completely clear, the following possible mechanism is described. A very large network is formed through polymerization due to the several functional groups in citric acid and PEI. Accordingly, a-CQDs are covalently bonded by amide (-NHCO-) linkages formed between carboxylic acid (-COOH) groups of citric acid and amine (-NH2) groups of PEI [37].

Fig. 2 A and B shows the FESEM images of a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposites, respectively. As can be seen, both nanocomposites have a spherical structure and are similar in size and shape. The average particle diameter of the synthesized nanocomposites a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs are 122 ± 17.4 and 119 ± 16.3 nm, respectively, which are not significantly different (p < 0.05). As mentioned earlier, the average size of a-CQDs was 4.9 nm. Therefore, the increase of nanocomposites size shows silica coatings on a-CQDs during the synthesis of nanocomposites [38].

The FTIR spectra of a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 2C. According to this figure, the bands at ∼470 and 800 cm−1 are related to O-Si-O and Si-O vibration, respectively. The band at ∼1050 cm−1 is also associated with the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si. The results confirm the formation of the silica layer on the a-CQDs [28]. As can be seen, the FTIR spectra of both nanocomposites are similar in main bands. It confirms the similar chemical structure of both nanocomposites and also the exit of tannic acid from a-CQDs/MIPs by washing. The OH/NH stretching vibration band in the nanocomposite a-CQDs/MIPs is more intense than that in a-CQDs/NIPs due to the hydrogen bonding between the template molecules and the monomers in a-CQDs/MIPs, which indicates the successful synthesis of MIP [39,40].

The role of ammonium hydroxide, cyclohexane, and Triton X-100 in the synthesis of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor was as the catalyst, continuous phase, and surfactant, respectively. Also, TA, APTES, and TEOS were used as template molecules, functional monomers, and cross-linker, respectively. Triton X-100 dispersed a-CQDs in cyclohexane. The a-CQDs should be coated with a layer of silica before the formation of MIP because the silica layer protects the a-CQDs and also improves the interaction between the a-CQDs and the MIP. The formation of the silica layer on the a-CQDs was done through the hydrolysis of TEOS, which was catalyzed by ammonium hydroxide. Then MIP was formed by copolymerization of APTES and TEOS in the presence of TA, during which hydrogen bonds were formed between APTES and TA [38].

Acetone was used to break the emulsion and separate the polymer from the mixture after MIP synthesis. Ethanol and acetonitrile were used to remove template molecules and unreacted functional monomers. The spectrum of UV–Vis absorption of the mixture extracted after each wash is shown in Fig. 3 A. As can be seen, the TA amount, which has an absorption wavelength of 276 nm, decreased after each wash. The TA molecule was not detectable after 5 washes. Also, the absorption peaks observed at 240 and 360 nm, which are related to unreacted a-CQDs, were decreased during washing. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite before washing was very low. However it increased dramatically after 5 washes. These results confirm the effective removal of TA and unreacted a-CQDs from nanocomposite a-CQDs/MIPs and also the synthesis of sensor with special detection sites.

The fluorescence emitted by a-CQDs is mainly due to the radiative recombination of the electron-hole pair on the surface. Blue fluorescence is generated at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm when the TA molecule is not present around the a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite. The fluorescence of the a-CQDs/MIPs sensor is quenched in the presence of TA. After TA addition, amine groups from APTES at the detection site of a-CQDs/MIPs react with TA functional groups such as hydroxyl to form a complex through the hydrogen bond that causes fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence quenching in MIPs is usually justifiable by two mechanisms: energy transfer and charge transfer [41]. The adsorption spectra of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor and TA are shown in Fig. 4 A to investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching. As shown in the figure, the absorption spectrum of the TA is close to the sensor gap band [38]. Fig. 4B shows the TA absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor. As shown in the figure, the TA adsorption spectrum has no overlap with the emission spectrum of a-CQDs/MIPs. Since the absorption spectrum of the quencher and the emission spectrum of the fluorophore must overlap in fluorescence quenching through energy transfer, it can be concluded that the cause of the fluorescence quenching of the a-CQDs/MIPs sensor is electron transfer. Normally, an electron is transferred from the valence band to the conduction band creates a hole in the valence layer when light is irradiated with an optimal excitation wavelength. The combination of this electron-hole pair causes fluorescence emission. But the binding of TA to a-CQDs/MIPs causes disturbance of radiative recombination of the electron-hole pair at a-CQDs. In this way, an electron from the TA enters the hole and prevents the return of the excited electron, which results in fluorescent quenching [28].

The response of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor is significantly affected by the pH of the environment (p < 0.05). The pH changes alter the charge of the polymer binding site of the MIP and the target molecule. As a result, acidic or alkaline environments affect the binding of a-CQDs/MIPs to the target molecule [42]. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the highest fluorescence quenching was obtained at pH = 7. In acidic environments, the hydrogen bond strength between APTES and TA decreased due to the protonation of cavities printed on a-CQDs/MIPs. On the other hand, the fluorescence quenching of the sensor decreased owing to the decomposition of the silica shell structure and surface defects in alkaline environments [43].

Results of analysis of variance showed that the amount of nanocomposite has a significant effect on the sensor response (p < 0.05). An excessive amount of a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite reduces the sensitivity of the target molecule detection, and an insufficient amount leads to a narrow linear range. As shown in Fig. 5B and 500 mg/L−1 of nanocomposite provided the highest fluorescence quenching. The sensor sensitivity decreased at a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite amounts lower than 500 mg/L−1 due to the very low fluorescence intensity of the sensor as well as the signal-to-noise ratio. The sensitivity of the sensor is proportional to the signal-to-noise ratio. On the other hand, the sensor sensitivity reduced at values above 500 mg/L−1 due to the fluorescence self-absorption phenomenon [44].

The response time indicates the time when the maximum fluorescence quenching is achieved by TA addition. Therefore, response time optimization was performed as one of the effective parameters in measuring TA from 0 to 240 s. As shown in Fig. 5C, response time has significant effect on the sensor response (p < 0.05). The sensor response increased along with the time increase to 120 s and after that, the response was not changed. Over time, the interaction between the TA and the MIP cavities increased, resulting in a higher fluorescence quenching. One of the advantages of the synthesized sensor is its short response time.

The effect of different TA concentrations on the fluorescence spectrum of a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 6 A and B, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs/MIPs decreased significantly by TA concentration increase, while it did not have much effect on the fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs/NIPs. It can be attributed to the specific interaction between TA and a-CQDs/MIPs due to the presence of imprinted holes on the surface of a-CQDs/MIPs, which results in more accurate concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching and therefore high sensitivity. However, there was only physical absorption of TA in a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposites, which leads to a very low sensitivity to TA concentration changes.

The plot of F0-F versus TA concentration was drawn to determine the calibration curve. Fig. 7 A and B shows the linear relationship between TA concentrations (1–200 nmol L−1) and the fluorescence intensities of a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs, respectively, under optimal conditions. As can be seen, a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite was much more sensitive to TA than a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposite d, ue to selective binding of a-CQDs/MIPs and TA. Although the a-CQDs/NIPs diagram was also linear, its linear equation compared to the a-CQDs/MIPs diagram linear equation showed very few fluorescence intensity changes to different TA concentrations, which reduces its accuracy and reproducibility.

The quenching amount of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor was evaluated according to the slope of the calibration curve (K). The K values for a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs were 4.2821 and 0.7758, respectively. The limit of detection of the sensor was also calculated to be 0.6 nmol L−1.

There is a positive correlation between the power of interaction and the IF. It means that the higher IF, the better the detection effect. IF of MIP was calculated to be 5.5. The high IF value of the synthesized polymer indicated its high selectivity.

The sensor response was measured for three different a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposites for TA concentration of 40 nmol L−1 to evaluate the reproducibility of the sensor. A RSD of 2.5% was obtained. In addition, the sensor response was measured for two concentrations of TA (n = 3) by the same a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite. The RSD was 4.03 and 4.5% for concentrations of 20 and 140 nmol L−1, respectively, indicating good reproducibility of the sensor. The stability of the sensor was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity changes of the a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite over time. Fig. 8 A shows the a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite response every 5 min for 1 h. The resulting plot indicates the excellent fluorescence stability of the sensor. The RSD of 3.2% was obtained.

The response of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor to TA in the presence of interfering species was evaluated to investigate the sensor selectivity. For this, the fluorescence intensity of a-CQDs/MIPs and a-CQDs/NIPs in the presence of TA (40 nmol L−1) and interfering species including glucose, sucrose, tartaric acid, citric acid, gallic acid, ascorbic acid, glycine, silver nitrate, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ (all at 200 nmol L−1) were recorded (Fig. 8B). The selectivity of a-CQDs/MIPs nanocomposite to TA was far better than a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposite. It is due to the presence of cavities on the a-CQDs, which are matchable with TA in terms of shape, size, and functional groups. On the other hand, the poor selectivity of a-CQDs/NIPs nanocomposites is due to the physical and non-selective binding of TA. The results show only a small difference in the sensor response in the presence of interfering species, which confirms its high selectivity.

The sensor was then used under optimal conditions to determine the TA concentration in real samples, including grape juice, black and green tea, using the standard addition method. The results are shown in Table 1 . As can be seen, the recovery values are between 97.4 and 103.6%, and the RSDs are in the range of 0.8–3.8%. The results show the excellent ability of a-CQDs/MIPs sensor in the detection of TA concentration in real samples.

Table 2 shows a comparison of the linear range and the detection limit of the a-CQDs/MIPs sensor prepared in this research with several methods reported for TA measurement. As can be seen, this method has a better detection limit than previous methods.

In this research, a fluorescence sensor was synthesized from a combination of a-CQDs and MIP technology to detect TA. First, a-CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Then, to improve the selectivity of the sensor, a layer of MIP was synthesized on the surface of a-CQDs by inverse microemulsion method. Under optimal conditions, the synthesized sensor had a linear response in the TA concentration range of 1–200 nmol L−1, and its detection limit was 0.6 nmol L−1. This method was successfully used to measure TA in grape juice, black and green tea samples. Results showed the synthesized sensor could be used as a rapid method for the detection of TA in food samples due to its desirable properties such as cheapness, simplicity of the synthesis method, low detection limit, high accuracy, and sensitivity. The lower sensor detection limit in this method is an advantage over other TA measurement methods. Another important benefit of the synthesized sensor over other methods is that it is not affected by complex matrices and interfering factors due to cavities proportional to the analyte.
Shabnam Sistani: Writing – original draft, Experiment, Software. Hajar Shekarchizadeh: Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) Research Council for financial support.
